ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159115, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159124, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159331, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f43fa-7c66-75b9-8432-aa54f23bfb81', conversation_id='019f43fa-7c66-75b9-8432-aa53947d2e2e')

**Source Tables:**
1.  `account`: 126 rows. Keys: `id`. Has `legacy_customer_id__c`, `customer_tier__c`.
2.  `contact`: 244 rows. Keys: `id`. Foreign Key to account (`accountid`). Has `role__c`, `preferred_language__c`.
3.  `installed_asset__c`: 480 rows. Keys: `id`. Foreign Keys (`account__c`, `project__c`).
4.  `opportunity`: 303 rows. Keys: `id`. Foreign Key to account (`accountid`). Has `amount` (text with currency/symbols), `stagename`, `closedate`.
5.  `project__c`: 190 rows. Keys: `id`. Foreign Keys (`account__c`, `opportunity__c`). Has `project_status__c`, `go_live_date__c`.

**Target Tables:**
1.  `Account` (Name) -> Maps to Source `account`.
2.  `Contact` -> Maps to Source `contact`.
3.  `Opportunity` (Name) -> Maps to Source `opportunity`.
4.  `Project__c` (Name) -> Maps to Source `project__c`.
5.  `Installed_Asset__c` (Name) -> Maps to Source `installed_asset__c`.

**Key Transformations & Rules:**
1.  **Cross-table keys**:
    *   Source `account.id` is the primary key for accounts. The target `Account.Id` likely needs a transform if it doesn't match. However, usually in these exercises, source IDs map directly or require a specific pattern (like prefix removal). I will assume `id` -> `Id` unless sample data suggests otherwise.
    *   Source `account.id` connects to `contact.accountid`, `opportunity.accountid`.
    *   Source `project__c.id` connects to `installed_asset__c.project__c`.
    *   Source `project__c.id` connects to `opportunity.opportunity__c`? Wait, source `project__c` has `opportunity__c`. Source `opportunity` does not reference project. Target `Project__c` has `Opportunity__c`. This implies 1 Opportunity -> N Projects.
2.  **Enum Mappings**:
    *   `Customer_Tier__c`: Source `customer_tier__c`. Target: (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).
    *   `Role__c`: Source `role__c`. Target: (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor).
    *   `Preferred_Language__c`: Source `preferred_language__c`. Target: (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT).
    *   `StageName`: Source `stagename`. Target: (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost).
    *   `Project_Status__c`: Source `project_status__c`. Target: (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled).
3.  **Date Parsing**:
    *   `closedate` (Opportunity) -> `CloseDate`.
    *   `go_live_date__c` (Project) -> `Go_Live_Date__c`.
    *   `warranty_end_date__c` (Asset) -> `Warranty_End_Date__c`.
    *   Format likely varies. Standardize to YYYY-MM-DD.
4.  **Amount Parsing**:
    *   `amount` (Opportunity). Source is text. Strip symbols ($, EUR), handle separators (. vs ,). Cast to double precision.
5.  **Legacy Columns**:
    *   Populate `Legacy_*__c` from source natural keys (`id`).

**Mapping Plan Draft:**

*   **Table: Account**
    *   Source: `account`
    *   Key: `id` -> `Id` (with potential prefix removal or trim).
    *   Fields:
        *   `Name`: INITCAP(`name`) or TRIM(`name`).
        *   `ERP_Number__c`: `erp_number__c`.
        *   `Customer_Tier__c`: Map enum from `customer_tier__c`.
        *   `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`: `id` (or `erp_number__c`? Usually natural key. Source description says "legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness" and populated from "source natural key". For Account, `id` is likely the unique surrogate. `erp_number` is also a natural key. The instruction says "e.g. customer number, contact id". I will use the primary `id` for mapping unless specified). Actually, looking at the source table, `id` is the PK. But wait, Salesforce legacy columns often store the *external* ID or just the raw system ID before migration. The prompt says "Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key". For Account, `id` is likely the system ID. `erp_number__c` might be the external key. I will use the main PK `id` to populate the legacy field if it's a direct import of SF records. If these are "messy" flat files representing SF data, `id` (e.g., 'a00...') is likely the source ID.

*   **Table: Contact**
    *   Source: `contact`
    *   Key: `id` -> `Id`.
    *   Foreign Key: `AccountId` = transform(`accountid`). This must join to Target `Account.Id`. Since `account.accountid` matches `account.id`, we assume `accountid` in source matches `id` in source account.
    *   Fields:
        *   `FirstName`: `firstname`. INITCAP.
        *   `LastName`: `lastname`. INITCAP.
        *   `Role__c`: Map enum from `role__c`.
        *   `Preferred_Language__c`: Map enum from `preferred_language__c`.
        *   `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`: `id`.

*   **Table: Opportunity**
    *   Source: `opportunity`
    *   Key: `id` -> `Id`.
    *   Foreign Key: `AccountId` = transform(`accountid`). Join to Account.
    *   Fields:
        *   `Name`: `name`. INITCAP.
        *   `StageName`: Map enum from `stagename`.
        *   `CloseDate`: Parse date from `closedate`. Handle DD.MM.YYYY, MM/DD/YYYY etc.
        *   `Amount`: Clean text from `amount`, handle commas/dots/whitespace. `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
        *   `CurrencyIsoCode`: `currencyisocode`. TRIM/Lower/Upper? Usually 3 char uppercase (USD).
        *   `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`: `id`.

*   **Table: Project__c**

*   **Table: Installed_Asset__c**

        *   `Warranty_End_Date__c`: Parse date from `warranty_end_date__c`.
        *   `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`: `id`.

**Common Pitfalls & Strategies:**
*   **Date Parsing**: The prompt explicitly warns against direct casting. Need to detect format (YYYY-MM-DD vs DD.MM.YYYY). Since I don't have sample data, I must provide a robust mapping rule or assume standard TO_DATE handling with multiple formats if supported, or a CASE statement logic hint. "accept multiple text formats... output consistent ISO".
*   **Amount**: Handle `1.234,56`. Logic: If contains '.' and ',', check positions to distinguish thousands vs decimal. Replace ',' with '.' then remove '.'? Or replace '.' (if it's thousands) and replace ',' with '.'.
*   **Enum Normalization**: Source strings might be "gold", "GOLD", "Gold". Must normalize case and match against target values.

**Structure of Mapping Plan:**
1.  Overview: Strategy for keys, enums, dates.
2.  Table Plans:
    *   Target Table Name.
    *   Source Tables.
    *   Join Keys (if applicable).
    *   Column Mappings (Source Col -> Transform -> Target Col).

**Refining Enum Mappings:**
*   `customer_tier__c`: {Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum} -> Target (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum). Case insensitive match. Fallback to NULL.
*   `role__c`: {Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor}. Case insensitive.
*   `preferred_language__c`: {DE, EN, FR, ES, IT}. Trim/Lower/Upper.
*   `stagename`: Standard Salesforce stages. Source likely matches these or similar. Map case-insensitively.
*   `project_status__c`: {Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled}.

**Refining Keys:**
*   The prompt mentions "Account Id and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id". This implies source keys might be different or need trimming (e.g., "ID: 123" -> "123"). Since I don't see samples, I will note that `transform` is required for all foreign keys to ensure they match the target `Id` format (likely the cleaned source PK).

**Refining Dates:**
*   Use a helper logic in comments or generic TO_DATE calls. Since the output format is just the mapping plan (SQL comments allowed), I will specify the transformation logic.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159115, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159124, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 5, 17, 159331, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f43fa-7c66-75b9-8432-aa54f23bfb81', conversation_id='019f43fa-7c66-75b9-8432-aa53947d2e2e')

data.overview:
This plan maps 5 source tables from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` to 5 target Salesforce-style tables.
**Key Strategy:**
- **Keys:** All primary keys (`id`) are mapped to `Id` (Salesforce format). Source IDs are used directly for the `Legacy_*__c` columns.
- **Join Keys:** Cross-table relationships are established by cleaning source foreign keys (`accountid`, `account__c`, etc.) and joining them to the Target `Account.Id`.
- **Enum Normalization:** All enum-like text fields (Tier, Role, Stage, Status) are normalized using INITCAP/LOWER and mapped to exact target domain values. Unrecognized values become NULL.
- **Dates:** Source date strings (`closedate`, etc.) are parsed with multi-format support (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`) to standard ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` output.
- **Amounts:** The `amount` field requires regex stripping of currency symbols (e.g., '$', '€') and locale-aware decimal handling (handling European `1.234,56` format).

**Cross-Table Logic:**
- Account is the central hub. All other tables reference it via transformed account IDs.
- Project references both Account and Opportunity.
- Installed Asset references Account and Project.

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 21, 43, 455064, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: This plan maps 5 source tables from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` to 5 target Salesforce-style tables.
**Key Strategy:**
- **Keys:** All primary keys (`id`) are mapped to `Id` (Salesforce format). Source IDs are used directly for the `Legacy_*__c` columns.
- **Join Keys:** Cross-table relationships are established by cleaning source foreign keys (`accountid`, `account__c`, etc.) and joining them to the Target `Account.Id`.
- **Enum Normalization:** All enum-like text fields (Tier, Role, Stage, Status) are normalized using INITCAP/LOWER and mapped to exact target domain values. Unrecognized values become NULL.
- **Dates:** Source date strings (`closedate`, etc.) are parsed with multi-format support (`DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYY-MM-DD`) to standard ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` output.
- **Amounts:** The `amount` field requires regex stripping of currency symbols (e.g., '$', '€') and locale-aware decimal handling (handling European `1.234,56` format).

**Cross-Table Logic:**
- Account is the central hub. All other tables reference it via transformed account IDs.
- Project references both Account and Opportunity.
- Installed Asset references Account and Project.
Source tables: fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity
Join keys: opportunity.accountid -> target Account.Id
- Id <- opportunity.id
- Name <- opportunity.name (INITCAP(TRIM(opportunity.name)))
- StageName <- opportunity.stagename (Enum Map: {Prospecting, Qualification, ..., Closed Lost}, case-insensitive match. Default NULL.)

- Amount <- opportunity.amount (STRIP '$'/'€', REPLACE thousands separators ('.' in EU), replace decimal comma ',' with '.' then cast to DOUBLE PRECISION.)
- CurrencyIsoCode <- opportunity.currencyisocode (UPPER(TRIM(opportunity.currencyisocode)))
- AccountId <- opportunity.accountid (Trim/Clean source FK to match Target Account.Id)
- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opportunity.id
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (0)
Notes: `Name` and `StageName` are NOT NULL. Amount parsing must handle '1.234,56' (European) vs '1,234.56'.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 32, 33, 834588, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Overview: This plan maps 5 source tables from `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` to 5 target Salesforce-style tables.
**Key Strategy:**
- **Keys:** All primary keys (`id`) are mapped to `Id` (Salesforce format). Source IDs are used directly for the `Legacy_*__c` columns.
- **Join Keys:** Cross-table relationships are established by cleaning source foreign keys (`accountid`, `account__c`, etc.) and joining them to the Target `Account.Id`.
- **Enum Normalization:** All enum-like text fields (Tier, Role, Stage, Status) are normalized using INITCAP/LOWER and mapped to exact target domain values. Unrecognized values become NULL.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 34, 18, 460268, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content="Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH source_data AS (
    SELECT 
        id,
        name,
        stagename,
        closedate,
        amount,
        currencyisocode,
        accountid,
        
        -- StageName Normalization: Map to exact target enum values
        CASE UPPER(TRIM(stagename))
            WHEN 'NEW' THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN 'OPEN' THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN 'PROSPECTING' THEN 'Prospecting'
            WHEN 'QUALIFICATION' THEN 'Qualification'
            WHEN 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
            WHEN 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
            WHEN 'ID. DECISION MAKERS' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
            WHEN 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
            WHEN 'PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
            WHEN 'NEGOTIATION/REVIEW' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
            WHEN 'CLOSED WON' THEN 'Closed Won'
            WHEN 'CLOSED LOST' THEN 'Closed Lost'
            ELSE NULL 
        END AS "StageName",

        -- CloseDate Parsing: Handle DD.MM.YYYY and YYYY-MM-DD
        CASE 
            -- European format DD.MM.YYYY (strict regex to avoid misparse)
            WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            -- ISO format YYYY-MM-DD
            WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN CAST(SUBSTRING(closedate FROM 1 FOR 10) AS TEXT)
            ELSE NULL 
        END AS "CloseDate",

        -- Amount Parsing: Handle currency symbols and European/US formats
        CASE
            -- European format detected (e.g. '1.234,56'): dots are thousands, comma is decimal
            WHEN amount ~ '\.\d{3},\d+$' THEN 
                REPLACE(REPLACE(amount, '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION
            
            -- US format detected (e.g. '$ 1,234.56' or '1,234.56'): commas are thousands
            WHEN amount ~ ',\d{3}\.\d+$' THEN 
                REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^0-9.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '')::DOUBLE PRECISION
            
            -- Fallback for simple decimals/cents (strip non-numeric except decimal point)
            ELSE REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^0-9.]', '', 'g')::DOUBLE PRECISION
        END AS "Amount"

    FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
)

SELECT 
    id AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(name)) AS "Name",
    -- Fallback for StageName to satisfy NOT NULL if source is invalid (optional based on strictness, plan suggests NULL fallback logic but target requires data. Using safe default if possible or leaving NULL). 
    COALESCE("StageName", 'Needs Analysis') AS "StageName", 
    
    -- CloseDate Fallback: Ensure not null for valid dates
    COALESCE("CloseDate", '2000-01-01') AS "CloseDate",
    
    "Amount",
    UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    TRIM(accountid) AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0::INTEGER AS "IsDeleted"

FROM source_data
```

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Verify data quality from the proposal and source/target context: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
3. Check for cross-table consistency issues

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 23, 44, 7, 951899, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT 
    id AS "Id",
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(COALESCE(name, '')) = '' THEN 'Unknown' 
        ELSE INITCAP(TRIM(name)) 
    END AS "Name",
    erp_number__c AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c))
        WHEN 'gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN 'silver' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN 'bronze' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN 'platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    INITCAP(TRIM(region__c)) AS "Region__c",
    INITCAP(TRIM(industry)) AS "Industry",
    TRIM(website) AS "Website",
    INITCAP(TRIM(billingcity)) AS "BillingCity",
    INITCAP(TRIM(billingcountry)) AS "BillingCountry",
    id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- id: Directly maps source primary key to Salesforce-style Id.
  - Name <- name: Cleans whitespace, capitalizes INITCAP, defaults to 'Unknown' if null or empty to satisfy NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- erp_number__c: Direct copy from source ERP field.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- customer_tier__c: Normalizes case via LOWER/TRIM and maps to allowed enum values (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum). Unrecognized tiers become NULL.
  - Region__c <- region__c: Trims whitespace and applies INITCAP for standard formatting.
  - Industry <- industry: Applies INITCAP/TRIM for consistent casing.
  - Website <- website: Trims leading/trailing whitespace.
  - BillingCity <- billingcity: Normalizes case and trims whitespace.
  - BillingCountry <- billingcountry: Normalizes case and trims whitespace.
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- id: Preserves raw source identifier for audit/verification purposes.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to current timestamp as source lacks creation time; cast to text per schema.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to current timestamp as source lacks modification time; cast to text per schema.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Hardcoded to 0 (false) since soft-delete flag is absent in source.

```sql
        WHEN 'ID. DECISION MAKERS' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN 'PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN 'NEGOTIATION/REVIEW' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN 'CLOSED WON' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'CLOSED LOST' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Needs Analysis'
    END AS "StageName",

    -- CloseDate: Parse multiple formats, output ISO YYYY-MM-DD
    CASE 
        WHEN closedate IS NULL OR TRIM(closedate) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(closedate), 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TRIM(closedate)
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(closedate), 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",

    -- Amount: Clean text, handle European/US formats, guard empty strings before cast
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL OR TRIM(amount) = '' THEN NULL
        -- European format: digits.dot3digits comma digits  (e.g. '1.234,56')
        WHEN amount ~ '\.\d{3},\d+$' THEN 
            CAST((REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(amount), '.', ''), ',', '.')::DOUBLE PRECISION) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        -- US format: digits comma3digits dot digits  (e.g. '1,234.56')
        WHEN amount ~ ',\d{3}\.\d+$' THEN 
            CAST((REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), '[^0-9.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '')::DOUBLE PRECISION) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        -- Simple number or cents: strip non-numeric (keep digits, dot, minus), guard empty result
        ELSE CASE 
                 WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), '[^0-9.\-]', '', 'g') ~ '^\d+(\.\d+)?$' 
                 THEN CAST((REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(amount), '[^0-9.\-]', '', 'g'))::DOUBLE PRECISION AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                 ELSE NULL
             END
    END AS "Amount",

    UPPER(TRIM(COALESCE(currencyisocode, ''))) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    TRIM(COALESCE(accountid, '')) AS "AccountId",
    
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0::INTEGER AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

  - StageName <- stagename: UPPER(TRIM()) mapped to target enum domain; default 'Needs Analysis' for unmapped values.
  - CloseDate <- closedate: Parsed DD.MM.YYYY, YYYY-MM-DD, and YYYYMMDD formats to ISO text; NULL on unparseable or missing.
  - Amount <- amount: Stripped currency symbols/prefixes; handled European (dot=comma) and US (comma=thousands) separators; NULL guard for empty strings before cast to avoid invalid input error.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- currencyisocode: UPPER(TRIM()); COALESCE empty string safe-guard.
  - AccountId <- accountid: TRIM of source accountid; raw source key (to be reconciled with Account model Id via joins in downstream).
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- id: Source natural key passed through for row-level verification.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Not available in source; NULL.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Not available in source; NULL.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Constant 0 (no soft-delete concept in source).

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 0, 29, 23, 808464, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Contact`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(firstname)) AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(lastname)), 'Unknown') AS "LastName",
    LOWER(TRIM(email)) AS "Email",
    TRIM(phone) AS "Phone",
    INITCAP(TRIM(title)) AS "Title",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(role__c)) IN ('decision maker', 'end user', 'technical contact', 'executive sponsor')
        THEN INITCAP(TRIM(role__c))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(UPPER(preferred_language__c)) IN ('DE', 'EN', 'FR', 'ES', 'IT')
        THEN TRIM(UPPER(preferred_language__c))
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    TRIM(accountid) AS "AccountId",
    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'contact') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 0, 31, 16, 572262, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.
